content.text: Circular motion is the motion of a body along a circular path. Even if the speed is constant, the velocity keeps changing because its direction changes continuously.
Uniform Circular Motion: Speed remains constant, but direction of velocity changes. Example: A satellite moving around Earth.
Non-Uniform Circular Motion: Both magnitude (speed) and direction of velocity change. Example: A car accelerating on a circular track.

content.name: Angular Displacement
content.note: Angle covered by the particle at the center. Unit: Radian (rad).

content.name: Angular Velocity
content.note: Rate of change of angular displacement. Unit: rad/s.

content.name: Angular Acceleration
content.note: Rate of change of angular velocity. Unit: rad/s².

content.text: 4. Relation Between Linear and Angular Quantities

content.name: Linear Relations
content.note: r = radius of the circular path.

content.name: Centripetal Force
content.note: Force required to keep a body moving in a circle. Always directed towards the center.

content.text: A pseudo force observed in a rotating frame. It acts away from the center and is equal in magnitude and opposite to centripetal force.

content.name: Banking Angle
content.note: Roads are tilted at an angle for safe turning and to prevent skidding.
